description: 6-archetype linear model for aiv_time, derived from msprof PipeUtilization measurements on 23 (model, batch) configs spanning BERT-base, GPT-2, HF-BERT (4-layer distilled w/ LM head), Net-Transformer (1-layer tiny classifier), Qwen3-0.6B (prefill + decode). v3 splits 'distilled' bucket into 'tiny_clf' (Net-Transformer-like, vocab*d small) vs 'distilled_lm' (HF-BERT-like, vocab*d large). The split matters because LM-head workload dominates AIV when vocab*d is large — Net-Transformer has α=1.2 vs HF-BERT α=5.6 despite both being layers<=4.
notes: v3 key insight: 'distilled' is two distinct archetypes by AIV behavior. Net-Transformer (1-layer + 1024-class tiny classifier) has α=1.20 because LM head is small. HF-BERT (4-layer + 30522-vocab LM head) has α=5.61 because LM head softmax dominates AIV vec work despite small body.

Net-Transformer specifically validated the user's observation: 'AIV operations have tiny latency but their MTE data-fetch dominates' — for small kernels, the AIV/AIC ratio stays close to 1 unless something amplifies it (e.g. big vocab softmax in HF-BERT).
Wave 3 added 4 Net-Transformer configs (b=1/4/8/16) — fixed ATC `--input_shape` requirement
malconv2/kitsune Wave 3 msprof collected but NOT in this fit (non-transformer architectures; ModelSpec doesn't apply)

Qwen3-S4096-b1 remains the largest residual outlier (33.5% err) — AIC archetype amp's `large` bucket median (5.42) under-shoots its actual (14.16) due to S-dependence; needs more S=4096+ samples to split.
